tldr: Both are Git-driven hosting platforms with PR previews, serverless and edge runtimes, so the migration is moderate rather than hard. Netlify tends to be cheaper at scale, ships a stronger plugin ecosystem (Build Plugins, Forms, Identity), and uses Deno-based Edge Functions; Vercel has deeper Next.js integration. Most of the work is translating vercel.json into netlify.toml, moving API routes to Netlify Functions, and re-pointing DNS.
why_switch: More predictable pricing at scale — Netlify's bandwidth and build-minute model often works out cheaper than Vercel Pro overages on bandwidth-heavy or image-heavy sites
Build Plugins ecosystem — declarative plugins for caching, sitemap, lighthouse, image optimization, and framework adapters that hook into the build lifecycle
Built-in product surface beyond hosting — Netlify Forms, Identity, and Blobs are first-party (Vercel pushes you to integrations)
Edge Functions on Deno — open-standard runtime (Web APIs, native TypeScript, npm via npm: specifiers) instead of Vercel's proprietary V8 isolate
Less framework gravity — Netlify treats Next.js, Astro, SvelteKit, Nuxt, Remix more uniformly via adapters; useful if you don't want a Next.js-first roadmap
Configuration as code — netlify.toml is committed by default and overrides UI settings, which is friendlier for IaC workflows than Vercel's UI-first model
Deploy Previews and branch deploys with a workflow you already know, so the day-to-day team UX doesn't regress
what_to_export: Git connection — note which repo, default branch, and which directory is the project root (monorepo subdir matters)
Build command and output directory — from Vercel project settings or vercel.json
Environment variables, scoped per environment (Production / Preview / Development) — pull with `vercel env pull` for each env
Custom domains and DNS records — apex/www config, wildcards, MX/TXT records
Redirects and rewrites — currently in vercel.json (`redirects`, `rewrites`, `headers`) plus any framework-level config
API routes / server functions — `/api/*`, Next.js Route Handlers, or any `/api` directory with serverless handlers
ISR / SSG configuration — which routes use revalidate, on-demand revalidation tags, and any fallback settings
Edge Middleware — `middleware.ts` at the project root and any `matcher` config
Image optimization config — `next.config.js` images block, remote patterns, formats
Preview deploy settings — branch deploy rules, password protection, deploy hooks
Team / users / access — roles per project, SSO config if any, webhook integrations
Cron jobs / scheduled functions — Vercel Cron entries in vercel.json
export_method: Pull a clean snapshot before changing anything. Run `vercel env pull .env.production` (and repeat for preview/development) to capture env vars. Commit a copy of `vercel.json` and `next.config.js` to a migration branch so you have a frozen reference. Document Vercel-specific behaviors that don't translate 1:1: ISR revalidate cadence, on-demand revalidation tags, image optimization (formats, remote patterns), Edge Middleware matchers, cron schedules, and any KV/Postgres/Blob storage you depend on. Export DNS zone records (apex, www, subdomains, MX/TXT) from your registrar — you'll re-point them after first successful Netlify deploy. Do NOT cancel the Vercel project until DNS has propagated and you've verified parity in production.
import_method: On Netlify, use Add new project -> Import an existing project, pick GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket, and select the repo. Netlify auto-detects framework, build command, and publish directory for known stacks; override on the import screen if needed. Translate `vercel.json` into a `netlify.toml` at repo root: `[build]` (command, publish, base for monorepos), `[[redirects]]` for redirects/rewrites, `[[headers]]` for headers, `[functions]` for the functions directory. Move API routes into `netlify/functions/` (Functions) or `netlify/edge-functions/` (Edge Functions). For Next.js apps, install the official Next.js adapter — it handles `next/image`, ISR, Route Handlers, and Middleware mapping automatically. Set environment variables under Site configuration -> Environment variables (or via `netlify env:import .env`). Verify a Deploy Preview, then point DNS to Netlify and decommission the Vercel project.

concept_mapping.to: netlify.toml (TOML, repo root, overrides UI settings)
Netlify Functions in netlify/functions/ (JS/TS/Go on AWS Lambda; us-east-2 by default for sites created after Oct 4, 2023)
Netlify Edge Functions in netlify/edge-functions/ (Deno runtime, Web APIs)
Netlify with the Next.js adapter (handles ISR) or On-demand Builders / build hooks for non-Next stacks
[[redirects]] blocks in netlify.toml (also supports a _redirects file)
Netlify environment variables with deploy-context scoping (production, deploy-preview, branch-deploy)
Netlify Blobs for object/blob-like data; bring-your-own for KV/Postgres (Upstash, Neon, Supabase, etc.)
Handled by the Netlify Next.js adapter for Next sites; otherwise Netlify Image CDN or a Build Plugin
Netlify Scheduled Functions (cron-style schedule on a Netlify Function)
Netlify Deploy Previews per PR + branch deploys (same model)
Netlify Analytics (server-side, paid add-on) or third-party RUM (Plausible, etc.)

effort: 3-10 days for a typical production app. Static sites and simple SPAs land in 1-2 days. Next.js apps with ISR, Middleware, image optimization, and cron jobs trend toward the upper end because each Vercel-specific feature needs verification on Netlify (often via the Next.js adapter). Add buffer for DNS cutover and re-testing third-party integrations bound to Vercel webhooks.
gotchas: Edge runtime difference — Vercel Edge runs on a V8 isolate with a Web-API-like surface; Netlify Edge Functions run on Deno. Most Web APIs work on both, but Node-only modules and some npm packages may need adjustment (Deno supports npm via `npm:` specifiers, but native addons won't work)

Next.js feature parity goes through the adapter — ISR, Route Handlers, Middleware, and next/image are supported on Netlify via the official Next.js adapter; verify the adapter version matches your Next.js version before assuming parity
ISR semantics — time-based revalidation generally works, but on-demand revalidation tags and any code that assumes Vercel's exact cache headers should be re-tested end-to-end
Image optimization — Vercel's built-in next/image pipeline isn't a 1:1 swap; on Netlify it goes through the adapter / Image CDN, and remote patterns and formats may need re-declaring

Environment variable scoping — Vercel scopes per env (Production/Preview/Development); Netlify scopes per deploy context (production, deploy-preview, branch-deploy, dev). Re-map carefully so previews don't accidentally hit production secrets
Middleware migration — Vercel Edge Middleware sits at the framework level; on Netlify it's an Edge Function with a path declaration. Matchers and request rewriting need to be ported, not lifted

Function limits differ — Netlify Functions default to 1024 MB memory, 60s synchronous / 15min background, 6 MB request/response payload (20 MB for streamed responses). If your Vercel functions rely on different limits, validate before cutover
Build minutes vs Vercel pricing — Netlify meters build minutes and bandwidth on its own usage model; rerun your cost estimate against actual traffic and build frequency before assuming savings

email.subject: Moving our frontend hosting from Vercel to Netlify
email.body:
Hey team,

I'm planning to move our frontend hosting from Vercel to Netlify. The reasoning: more predictable pricing at our traffic level, a stronger plugin ecosystem (Forms, Identity, Build Plugins), and Deno-based Edge Functions instead of Vercel's proprietary edge runtime. Both platforms are Git-driven with PR previews, so the day-to-day workflow won't change.

Migration plan at a high level:
  1. Import the repo into Netlify (Add new project -> Import from Git).
  2. Translate vercel.json into netlify.toml (build, redirects, headers, functions).
  3. Move /api routes to netlify/functions and Edge Middleware to netlify/edge-functions; for Next.js the official adapter handles most of this (ISR, next/image, Route Handlers).
  4. Re-create environment variables per deploy context (production / deploy-preview / branch-deploy) using `netlify env:import`.
  5. Verify a Deploy Preview end-to-end, then re-point DNS and decommission the Vercel project.

Realistic effort: 3-10 days depending on how much we lean on Vercel-specific features (ISR tags, Edge Middleware, image optimization, cron). Main risks to validate: Edge runtime differences (V8 isolate vs Deno), ISR parity through the adapter, and env-var scoping. I'll flag anything that doesn't map cleanly so we can decide before cutover.

Let me know if you want me to spike a Deploy Preview first or go straight into the full plan.
